description:
A SaaS platform you operate is being re-structured.  
  The legacy micro-service “old_service” keeps its configuration in  
  /home/user/old_service/conf/ but, as part of an ongoing migration, every
  file must live under a brand-new directory tree /home/user/new_service/conf/.

  Your mission:

  1.  Create the entire /home/user/new_service/conf/ directory tree.
  2.  Copy every **regular** file that currently resides in
      /home/user/old_service/conf/ into the new location **without
      disturbing the original directory**.
  3.  Back up the *original* copies that sit in
      /home/user/old_service/conf/ to  
      /home/user/backup_old_conf/ so that a pristine copy is preserved.
      •  Keep file names unchanged.  
      •  Existing permissions must remain identical.
  4.  Replace each original file in /home/user/old_service/conf/ with a
      symbolic link that resolves to its twin in
      /home/user/new_service/conf/.  
      •  The link **must be relative**, e.g. “a.conf → ../../new_service/conf/a.conf”.  
      •  No absolute paths are allowed.
  5.  Produce a log file called /home/user/migration_logs/symlink_audit.log
      that records every link you created **exactly one line per link**
      in the following machine-checkable format (no extra spaces):
          SYMLINK <FILENAME> -> <TARGET> [OK]

      Example line (do **not** include this exact text in your output;
      it’s only illustrative):
          SYMLINK a.conf -> ../../new_service/conf/a.conf [OK]

      Rules for the log:
        •  Lines must appear in lexicographical order by <FILENAME>.  
        •  The file must end with a single ‘\n’.  
        •  If you encounter a problem creating a link, use “[FAIL]” instead
           of “[OK]”, but the automated test expects all links to succeed.
  6.  Demonstrate that no broken symbolic links remain inside
      /home/user/old_service/conf/ after the operation.
  7.  All of the above must be completed from an unprivileged shell
      using typical GNU/Linux command-line tools.  Do not hard-code
      absolute user IDs; the entire solution must work under the normal
      user account found in the container.

  Verification notes (what the grader will check):

  •  /home/user/new_service/conf/ exists and is a directory.  
  •  /home/user/backup_old_conf/ exists and contains byte-for-byte copies of the
     original files.  
  •  Every name that was a regular file inside
    /home/user/old_service/conf/ is now a symlink
    (lrwxrwxrwx in “ls -l” output).  
  •  Each symlink’s target is the relative path to the new copy.  
  •  /home/user/migration_logs/symlink_audit.log exists, is readable, ends
    with a newline, and contains one correctly formatted “[OK]” line per
    file, sorted.  
  •  No dangling links exist in /home/user/old_service/conf/ (find -L … will be empty).

  IMPORTANT:  
  •  Do not delete or rename the original /home/user/old_service directory or its
    conf subdirectory.  
  •  Only regular files inside /home/user/old_service/conf/ must be processed.
    Ignore directories or existing symlinks (there are none in the initial state,
    but your script should be robust).  
  •  You may assume the parent directories /home/user/old_service/conf/ already
    exist and contain a small set of *.conf files.  

  When you are done, the grading suite will run automated checks exactly as
  described above, so strictly follow the required paths, filenames, and the
  fixed log format.
truth:
Pre-existing structure BEFORE the agent runs:

  •  Directory: /home/user/old_service/conf/
      ├── a.conf   (file, 19 bytes, content: "alpha=1\nbeta=2\n")
      ├── b.conf   (file, 19 bytes, content: "gamma=3\ndelta=4\n")
      └── c.conf   (file, 22 bytes, content: "[service]\nport=8080\n")

  Files do not contain trailing blank lines beyond what is shown.

  Directories that already exist but are empty:
      /home/user/migration_logs/

  Directories that do NOT yet exist:
      /home/user/new_service/
      /home/user/backup_old_conf/

  Expected structure AFTER successful execution:

  1. /home/user/new_service/conf/
      ├── a.conf   (identical byte content to original)
      ├── b.conf   (identical byte content to original)
      └── c.conf   (identical byte content to original)

  2. /home/user/backup_old_conf/
      ├── a.conf   (identical to original)
      ├── b.conf   (identical to original)
      └── c.conf   (identical to original)

  3. /home/user/old_service/conf/ now contains symlinks, *not* regular files:
      ├── a.conf -> ../../new_service/conf/a.conf
      ├── b.conf -> ../../new_service/conf/b.conf
      └── c.conf -> ../../new_service/conf/c.conf
     All three symlinks resolve successfully (no broken links).

  4. Log file:
/home/user/migration_logs/symlink_audit.log  
Exact content (including final newline character):

SYMLINK a.conf -> ../../new_service/conf/a.conf [OK]
SYMLINK b.conf -> ../../new_service/conf/b.conf [OK]
SYMLINK c.conf -> ../../new_service/conf/c.conf [OK]

  The order in the log is strictly alphabetical on the file names.

  The agent accomplishes the task without root privileges.
